speaker: interviewer
text: Edith Abrahams, tape four, October the 18, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I said we changed vocations. Actually, I worked in a leather goods store. But that's not the change of vocation I'm talking about. I had a nice job and became assistant buyer. The vocation was right next door to, the-- it was called The Traveler's Shop, was the Stage Door Canteen, which was started by Bob Hope. And this is where I met Hal. I was an elevator operator, volunteer, of course. And I met him, and we clicked. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about Hal briefly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hal is your husband of almost 50 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he-- he had been wounded in the war. He had been wounded in Leyte. And he had come to Cleveland. He was at a hospital. He had a cast on that weighed 25 pounds. And his arm was up in the air. He looked like the Statue of Liberty, because of his-- he had no shoulder joint. A sniper had gotten him into the shoulder. And this is why his arm was up. And the arm was gradually being lowered, because at that time, they didn't have any kind of implements they put in the way they do now. The shoulder joint was solid. And in order to-- this to fuse, it took time, and they had to gradually. And when we finally got engaged-- we met June 1945. And we got married February 3, 1946. By the time that the cast came off was when we got engaged. And his arm was out like that. But he has full use of the arm now. We moved to-- to New Jersey. And we lived in Irvington. And we had a one little furnished apartment. And we lived there. We got-- after we got married, we had our first child. Three years later, Joanie was born in 1949. And then two years after that, Susan was born in 1951. And then number three, Randi was born in 1955. And the kids grew up-- two of them grew up in Orange first. They were living in Orange. Then we moved to Livingston, New Jersey. And we bought a house. And we lived there over 30 years. And each one of the kids, they found their own way. And they live in different parts of the country. They live in New Jersey. The youngest one lives in New Jersey. Randi lives in New Jersey. The middle one lives in Indianapolis. And the oldest one, Joanie, lives in Arizona. They're happily married and they have children. Joanie has a daughter named Ryley. She's a teenager now. And Susan has two boys, Zachary and Ethan. And he was born first. He was the first one. And then Randi has two girls, Shari and Jessica. But in the first-- the first year, the children, they were all born in the first year. First came Zachary, and then came Shari, and then came Jessica. And I was pretty proud to have three grandchildren born in one year. And also the--

speaker: interviewer
text: They're all very artistic from what I have heard. And I think they get a lot of that from you. I would like you to show this and talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a book called And this was written at the time when I was teaching at a temple. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tilt it back? Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This way?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had so many requests for the type of thing that I was teaching. I was working with my closest friend who was an artist, who was also interested in the arts, Carol Tauben. And we-- we co-authored this book. And it has a great deal of meaning. You know, I was very excited, because it's being used at temples all over the country. And it teaches-- it's not just an arts and crafts book, but it's a way of-- a three dimensional way of teaching holidays and ethics and a great many things that are relevant in today's world. And it's a step by step guide, but it's really emphasizing what we're trying to say in Judaism. And I'm very proud of this. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you have instilled in your children a love of art and have given them many professions. And I'd like you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, Joanie, the oldest one, she's an art teacher. She's teaches in junior high. And she was picked as teacher of the year of her school. And she was just chosen recently, the state of Arizona notified her, she's one of 10 who was picked as a teacher of the year for the state, which is very exciting. Susan teachers animation in clay to children. She's also an artist on her own. And certainly, Joanie also, she's an artist on her own. She does these huge paintings. And Susan is in-- her work is in the Smithsonian Institute. She has a small triplane, a ceramic triplane there. And now, she teaches animation in clay. Randi has shown her creativities in other ways. But she's was an x-ray technician-- technician. That was her field.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are an artist now, because we are sitting amongst paintings and sculptures you have done. And where do you-- where did you first become so artistic or expressed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always liked-- it was something that I liked and enjoyed. And it came about when in Germany, even as a little girl, we had a kinder fraulein, in other words a maid, who had been a nursery school teacher, a kindergarten teacher. And she had wakened in me the love for art and miniaturism. Because she made very tiny things. And she taught me how to do that. And I loved it. And she also taught me a pretty appreciation of trying out different media. And that's why I'm interested in not just working with one media. Painting, there's one of my paintings here. Sculpture, this is one of mine. But it also carried itself out in another way. I decided to work with puppets. And Hal and I, we create our own puppets. We put on puppet shows throughout the Southern Orange County. And it's because I miss the children when I was teaching the children when I was working in the temple and teaching class at the Jewish Community Center. I miss the kids. And we missed our grandchildren, because we're far away from them. And this way, we're dealing with children. And the love of children is very important to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before we introduce Hal and your puppets that you share in common now, I would like you to think about what you would like to tell your children, your grandchildren, and the world, what you have learned and what you want the world to learn from your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had gone back to Frankfurt, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, please tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We had-- the City of Frankfurt had invited us. And I had an experience there that taught me that not everything is all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were there in 1992. And one experience we had was very strong in that respect. And it came about really through puppets. Because I had gone back to the apartment where we had lived and met someone who lived there who discussed puppetry with me and led Hal and myself to a friend of theirs who had been a puppeteer. And we were invited to sit in their kitchen. And there was someone sitting in the corner. And the woman that had invited us into the kitchen asked me about my background. And I began to tell her a little bit about Nazism. And she said she was particularly interested, because she had seen a program called Shoah. And I-- she asked me if I had seen the program. And I said, oh, yes. Of course. I didn't even know it was shown in Germany. And at that moment, the woman-- and, of course, the conversation was completely in German. Hal didn't understand it. He doesn't understand German. She's announced to me that Hitler wasn't so bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I questioned her, why did she feel that way? And she said, well, if he hadn't started with the Jews, nothing would have. Because he was a very good man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it dawned on me that this was a Nazi sitting, and it was literally bizarre. It's 1992. I'm invited into a house, and there's a Nazi. And I thought I was going to explode. I was so upset. And I said I had to leave. And then the woman that had invited us in explained that this was her mother. And she was very sorry that this had happened. But she gave me a clue that this is not dead, that a lot of things like that are still happening in Germany. And in fact, we met with the youth group at that point. I was being interviewed. And I was curious how they felt. Before they interviewed me, I wanted their feeling. I needed to know had they discussed that with their parents and grandparents. And they said they had no communication with their parents and grandparents. And-- but I then discovered that the Germans had not-- are not teaching the kids in school what they should be teaching them about that terrible time that they had perpetrated. They teach the kids that in a sort of offhand way when they are more or less grown already, when they're like 17 years old, where it should be in the school system.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was refreshing that this youth group felt very strongly. They had-- but they were teenagers already. But they used the words 'never again,' which was something that we use.

speaker: interviewee
text: But coming out of there with that, my feeling is that we must be vigilant. That we must not think that this cannot happen again. Because I feel even in an innocent way, we must be very careful that bigotry and racism raises its ugly head. If a joke is told that goes in that direction, one should watch out. Because I feel it can certainly happen again. And we have to be very, very careful. And this is my feeling for anyone who comes up in the world today.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we'll call. We'll get Hal.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband Hal, Harold Abrahams. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is one of our latest additions, our puppets from Russia. This is Nikolai and Misha.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, guys. Let's [INAUDIBLE]. There's no real music now. [SINGING] You've got a sample of what we usually do. We have a professional show with 23 puppets. And these are the new guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the new addition to Puppets From Around the World. We have puppets from every country and every country-- every puppet has its own original costume and music to go with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're married almost 50 years. We will be married 50 years. That's it for these guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that [? enough? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The upper part in the picture is my dad, my mother, and my two sisters. In the middle is Renata, who became Renee. The middle is Margot, and then it's me over on the left. That's it. Below is my family now. In the middle is Hal and I. And on the left, my three girls. Joanie is in the middle on the bottom there. And Susan is top-- top, no above, right there. And Randi's over there. And there are three-- I have five grandchildren and all three son in-laws, Alan and Phil and Mark. And the book is called This is a book, not just a picture. by Edith Abrahams.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandparents' golden wedding anniversary, their 50th anniversary, in 193-- January 16, 1935. I am right behind my grandmother with a little bow on the-- on the dress. My mother's behind me. And there's the rest of the family. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Jewish school called the Philanthropin in Frankfurt in 1804 to  1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a photo of my class in the school at the Philanthropin. I'm in the left-hand sitting down in the corner. And some of these gals I saw again in Frankfurt from the-- at the reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our leather goods store, the one that the boycott occurred on April 1, 1933. My sister is at the register, my father the left-hand corner there, talking to the customer. And one of those briefcases you're looking at is probably the one that he used to smuggle the money out of Germany into France. This is a photo of my mom and dad on the day that my dad left for France when he was fleeing. This was August 13, 1933. On the porch up above and at the station, it's a very stressful day for all of us when we were-- when they were-- when my dad was leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the restaurant that we opened after we had returned from France and Holland called Speisehaus Wolf. You see the Star of David up above? That's what the sign had. Below us-- that was the calling-- that's the card, business card. Below us is the actual restaurant. And on the left is the three of us. My sister Margot is on the left wearing the apron, Renata before she became Renee is in the middle. And I got very tall then and skinny. And I'm on the right. And this was at-- right at the [INAUDIBLE] downtown Frankfurt. And the date is sometimes in 1935. This is my passport. You can see the emblem there. Now, there you could see the red J in it with the [NON-ENGLISH]. That's the famous one and my name. This is actually not when I was 18. It was a younger picture. I think I was 14 on the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we see that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just took any picture and stuck it in there. And interesting, they said on one of the pages it tells you that it would be invalidated after July 1939. I had not planned to return. It shows that we left on October 29, 1938. It was exactly two weeks before the Kristallnacht, the Night of the Broken Glass. We were the lucky ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This was my Aunt Lily, Lily Frankfurter, on the ship the There's a sad story about this ship. This was the day they landed in Belgium. They made a movie. And they wrote a book called And she's right in the middle of the picture with a circle around. I created that circle. [INAUDIBLE] Hal and our engagement. We were at an aunt's house. That's Hal and I. And this was in 1945. OK. These are our diamonds, our three beautiful daughters. On the left is our Susan, the middle daughter. In the middle is Joanie, our oldest daughter. And on the right is Randi. They're very special. These are our three daughters. On the left is Randi with her two girls. And way over on the left is Jessica, the younger one, then comes Shari. Next to Shari is Joanie, our oldest daughter. And on her lap is Ryley. And next to Ryley is Susan, our middle daughter, and her two boys. On her lap is Ethan. And next to that is Zachary, we call him Zach. But this was taken a couple years ago, so the kids are much taller now. This was taken in Frankfurt when Hal and I were invited back and was at the cemetery. This is a double grave. On the right, my grandmother. And on the left, my grandfather who died in Theresienstadt. He's not buried in that grave, but the grave is to remind us that he's-- he died there. And also, I discovered when I was in Frankfurt that my boyfriend that I had talked about, he died in a concentration camp. He barely made it-- he didn't make it. He died in 1944 just before liberation in a camp called Flossenburg. It was another camp. But he was on the plaque that I discovered in Frankfurt. And he's also in the-- in the lists that the Germans kept so carefully.
